title: Microwave Studies of Single Crystal TeO2 at Cryogenic Temperatures
abstract: We use whispering-gallery-mode analysis to characterise the microwave dielectric properties of single-crystal TeO$_2$ at cryogenic temperatures and compare its loss performance with other low-loss dielectric materials. Finite-element modelling is combined with measurements at room temperature, 4 K, and 20 mK to develop accurate cryogenic simulations and extract the anisotropic dielectric permittivities, giving $\varepsilon_\parallel=25.75\pm0.08$ and $\varepsilon_\perp=20.90\pm0.07$. Loss measurements reveal quality factors as high as $9\times10^6$ and minimum loss tangents approaching $3\times10^{-8}$, placing TeO$_2$ among promising low-loss dielectrics for cryogenic microwave applications. Electron-spin-resonance spectroscopy further indicates a clean spin environment, while identifying distinct spin systems consistent with the known properties of the crystal.

bite.hook: Single-crystal TeO2 cooled to 20 mK achieves an ultra-low microwave loss tangent approaching 3e-8.
tldr: They characterized the microwave dielectric properties and loss performance of single-crystal tellurium dioxide (TeO2) at cryogenic temperatures.
They combined finite-element modeling with whispering-gallery-mode measurements from room temperature down to 20 millikelvin.
They measured quality factors up to 9 million and extracted highly precise, anisotropic dielectric permittivities for the crystal.
bite.whyItMatters: Quantum technologies and precision detectors require materials that do not absorb or waste microwave energy. Demonstrating that TeO2 has exceptionally low loss at ultra-cold temperatures makes it a prime candidate for building high-performance cryogenic devices.
bite.eli5: When you ring a bell, it eventually stops vibrating because energy is lost. The researchers cooled a special crystal called tellurium dioxide to near absolute zero and found that it can 'ring' with microwave energy for an incredibly long time without losing its signal.

keyNumbers.label: Parallel dielectric permittivity
keyNumbers.context: Measured anisotropic dielectric permittivity parallel to the crystal axis at cryogenic temperatures (with an uncertainty of 0.08).

keyNumbers.label: Perpendicular dielectric permittivity
keyNumbers.context: Measured anisotropic dielectric permittivity perpendicular to the crystal axis at cryogenic temperatures (with an uncertainty of 0.07).

keyNumbers.label: Maximum quality factor
keyNumbers.context: The highest quality factor (Q) observed during cryogenic microwave loss measurements of the TeO2 crystal.

keyNumbers.label: Minimum loss tangent
keyNumbers.context: The lowest limit of microwave energy loss measured in the TeO2 crystal at cryogenic temperatures.

glossary.shortDef: A wave resonance that circulates continuously around a concave or circular boundary with very low loss.
glossary.eli5Def: Waves of light or microwaves trapped in a circle, bouncing around the edges like sound traveling around a circular dome.

glossary.shortDef: A measure of how easily a material polarizes in response to an electric field.
glossary.eli5Def: How much a material resists or permits an electric field passing through it.

glossary.shortDef: A parameter quantifying the electromagnetic energy dissipation in a dielectric material.
glossary.eli5Def: A measure of how much electrical energy gets wasted as heat when passing through a material.
